Short description: Use DAISY to test whether structured disclosure changes what authors report about AI use and how they experience responsibility for it.
Empirical unit of analysis: AI disclosure statements produced with and without structured support
Candidate roles tested: Accountability and labor allocator; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Are disclosure and provenance architecture distinct roles in practice, or two sides of the same governance function?
Observable artifacts / traces: Disclosure forms, generated statements, completeness scores, participant preferences
Key outcomes observed: Disclosure completeness, author comfort, policy alignment, disciplinary differences
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Structured disclosure does not change what authors report or how complete reports become
Main confounds / risks of misreading: The case studies disclosure, not truthfulness of underlying self-report
Why this case has high leverage: Good test of whether a role is about reflective accountability or just compliance.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
